# **RL-Based Strategies for Improving Synthetic CAPTCHAs**

## **Report 5: Architecture & Implementation**

**CMPE 195B \- Senior Design Project II** **Spring 2026**

---

**Team Members:**

| Name | SJSU Email |
| ----- | ----- |
| Meghana Indukuri | meghana.indukuri@sjsu.edu |
| Eman Naseerkhan | eman.naseerkhan@sjsu.edu |
| Martin Tran | vietnhatminh.tran@sjsu.edu |

**Project Advisor:** Dr. Younghee Park

**Date:** 3/27/2026

## 

## **1\. System Architecture** 

**![][image1]**Figure 1: System architecture of the RL-based CAPTCHA system

**Frontend:** The frontend of this environment contains the UI components that the user interacts with. It also displays the CAPTCHA challenge that the user interacts with to prove they are human. User interaction with the environment is tracked as user telemetry during each purchasing session. Telemetry tracks mouse movement, scrolling and clicks as well as keyboard key presses.

![][image2]

Figure 2: Environment Architecture

**Backend:** The backend contains the REST API for the application and the logic for user checkouts and telemetry ingestion for the agent service. When the user triggers a checkout, their telemetry session ends and the agent is given the final data entries and then queried for a decision on the status of the user. The agent service ingests the user telemetry over the course of a user session and assigns the probability of a human or bot user based on the RL agent and classifier training. When a checkout is triggered by the frontend, the agent is signaled to make a decision on the status of the user and returns the result to the frontend to present a challenge if a bot was detected.  
**Database:** The database for this environment saves the raw telemetry in each session. This telemetry data is accessed by the agent to make decisions and is saved for future training sessions as needed.

## **2\. Implementation Details** 

**Technology stack:**

* Frontend: React 18.2, Vite 5, React Router DOM 6, vanilla CSS  
* Backend: Python 3.12, Flask 3.0, Flask-CORS, mysql-connector-python  
* Agent: PyTorch PPO+LSTM (lazy-loaded, thread-safe), sklearn, XGBoost  
* Database: MySQL 8.0+  
* Bot Tooling: Selenium 4.15+, browser-use (CDP), Playwright 1.40+  
* LLM Integration for Bot: Anthropic (Claude Sonnet 4), OpenAI (GPT-4o), and Gemini (Gemini 3 Flash) via browser-use

**Key Algorithms:**

* RL Agent:  
  * PPO \+ LSTM (Proximal Policy Optimization with LSTM)  
    * The primary RL agent uses a clipped surrogate policy gradient method. It trains an \`LSTMActorCritic\` network (128 hidden units, 1 layer) over windowed event sequences.  
  * DG+LSTM (Delightful Gradients with LSTM)  
    * A deterministic policy variant: temperature parameter (η, default \= 1.0) and blends deterministic and stochastic PPO behavior (released in 2026\)  
  * Soft PPO (Adaptive Entropy)  
    * Learns entropy coefficient (α) dynamically, better for off-policy learning  
    * Target entropy ratio: 0.5  
    * α learning rate: 3×10⁻⁴  
* Classification Model  
  * XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting)  
    * Session-level binary classifier:  
      * Features: 39 handcrafted  
      * Estimators: 200  
      * Max depth: 3  
      * Learning rate: 0.05  
      * L1: 0.3, L2: 2.0  
      * Label smoothing: 0→0.05, 1→0.95

**Data models:** Table 3 shows the data model for tracked user telemetry. This data is tracked from the start of each user session until the user clicks checkout, where the data is flushed to the agent, and a decision is made on the probability of the user being a human or a bot.

| Signal | Rate | Data |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Mouse Movement | 15 ms sampling | Position (x, y), Timestamp |
| Mouse Clicks | Every click | Position (x, y), Timestamp, Target Element, Time Delta, Button |
| Mouse Scrolls | Every scroll | Scroll X, Scroll Y, Delta, Time Delta, Timestamp |
| Key Strokes | Every key press | Field ID, Type (Up / Down), Timestamp, Special Keys (Backspace, Tab, etc...) |

Table 1: Tracked User Telemetry

| Column | Type |
| ----- | ----- |
| session\_id | VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY |
| session\_start | TIMESTAMP |
| page | VARCHAR |
| mouse\_movements | JSON |
| click\_event | JSON |
| keystroke\_data | JSON |
| scroll\_events | JSON |
| form\_completion\_time | JSON |
| browser\_info | JSON |
| session\_metadata | JSON |

Table 2: user\_sessions Schema

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| /api/health | GET | Health check |
| /api/checkout | POST | Save checkout |
| /api/tracking/mouse | POST | Mouse telemetry |
| /api/tracking/clicks | POST | Click telemetry |
| /api/tracking/keystrokes | POST | Keystrokes telemetry |
| /api/tracking/scroll | POST | Scroll telemetry |
| /api/agent/rolling | POST | Rolling inference |
| /api/agent/evaluate | POST | Full evaluation |
| /api/agent/confirm | POST | Online learning update |
| /api/agent/dashboard/{sid} | GET | Debug dashboard |
| /api/agent/live/{sid} | GET | Live stats |
| /api/agent/sessions | GET | Session list |
| /api/agent/session-ids | GET | Session IDs |
| /api/session/raw/{sid} | GET | Raw telemetry |
| /api/export/tracking | GET | Export telemetry |
| /api/export | GET | Export checkouts |

Table 3: Backend API Endpoints

## **3\. Development Retrospective**

**What worked:**  
Overall, our environment development has been successful. This has benefited from our early development of our prototype, which was done in September. Shortly after this was completed we moved into developing our application. Starting early and having a working project has allowed us to spend much of this semester focusing on optimizing our application. The planning and management for the development was also done well. Splitting into two groups, one for the web application and one for the RL model, is what allowed us to begin early. The RL group initially focused on research while the application group began developing, so the development process was not dependent on the research being completed for all aspects.   
**Challenges overcome:**  
	With both the primary RL agent and classification model we struggled with the results. Both had perfect success rates, which should not be occurring. Overcoming this challenge took a lot of trial and error as multiple elements influence this. We first considered that the application may be too simple and so the data is limited, so we added more pages and fields for interaction. The issue continued so we then focused on improving the bots by using different API’s for the LLM bots and including jitter in bots to make them more human-like. This was successful, and during this process we also noticed that adding more data improved the issue, making data collection a high priority for us.   
**Lessons learned:**  
	The RL part of our project requires a significant amount of data to get good results from. We anticipated this, however we did not create a plan or process to collect data consistently throughout the project. This resulted in some tasks being slowed down as they were waiting for data from other parts of the project before they could continue.  
Vital components in the project should be thoroughly planned around; otherwise, some work may need to be redone. A good example of this for our project is data collection for our project. As stated before, we need a significant amount of data for our project to function properly. Collecting this data involved time-intensive work like manually interacting with the environment or running bots through it. Our frontend went through a few different iterations before we were happy with it. This invalidated some of our previously collected data, further restricting the effectiveness of our agent. If we had arrived at a final frontend design earlier in our project cycle, we would likely have more data to work with.
